result:
The moon is a liar.

It hangs there every night, pretending to be gentle silver, pretending to be constant, pretending to be the same moon that watched wolves pad across frozen tundra and guided ships across black oceans. But it’s a trick. The moon we see is just the echo of something that happened a second and a half ago—light bouncing off a rock that is, at this exact moment, already in a different place than where we’re looking. We fall in love with a ghost.

I keep thinking about that while I sit in the fluorescent hum of an all-night laundromat that smells like burnt dryer sheets and someone else’s leftover cologne. My socks are spinning in a machine that thumps like a drunk heartbeat, and I’m scribbling this on the back of a receipt for two energy drinks and a pack of cinnamon gum. The cashier who rang me up had a tattoo of a paper airplane behind her ear; when I asked why, she said, “So my thoughts can always find the exit.” I haven’t stopped thinking about that either.

Outside, the parking lot is a shattered mirror of potholes and oil rainbows. A stray cart has made it half-way to the road, frozen mid-escape, like it changed its mind. Everything is in between: the cart, the moon, me. We’re all stuck being the version of ourselves that light is still catching up to.

I used to believe growing up was a straight line—some secret math that, once solved, would unlock the next level, like in a video game. Instead it’s more like this laundromat: you bring in a tangled mess, dump it into something that rattles and steams, and hope whatever comes out is at least marginally cleaner and doesn’t shrink. Sometimes you forget a sock and it becomes a stray piece of someone else’s life. Sometimes the machine eats a quarter and you stand there feeling robbed by a metal box that will never apologize.

There’s a woman two rows over folding tiny shirts the color of Easter eggs. She hums while she works, some tune I almost recognize—maybe a lullaby, maybe a hymn, maybe both. Each shirt she snaps open hangs for a second in the air like a prayer flag before it lands perfectly square in the pile. I wonder whose tiny shoulders will wear them, what grass stains and finger paints and first-day-of-school pictures are already queued up in the fabric’s future. I wonder if she’s folding memories she hasn’t lived yet, the same way I’m folding words about a moon that isn’t where I think it is.

I read once that every atom in our bodies was cooked in the furnace of a star that exploded before Earth existed. That means the calcium in my teeth, the iron in my blood, the sodium lighting up my neurons—all of it is stardust that learned how to be lonely. So when I look at the moon, I’m really looking at cousin debris that took a different exit ramp. We’re all just cosmic shrapnel trying to remember the explosion.

The dryer clicks off. My socks are warm, still slightly damp, rolled into each other like sleeping ferrets. I stuff them into the backpack that’s carried me through three states and two breakups and one night in a Greyhound station where a man played “Here Comes the Sun” on a harmonica until security made him leave. The zipper sticks the way it always does, as if arguing that nothing should be allowed to leave this place unchanged.

Outside, the moon has slipped lower, a silver coin balanced on the horizon’s thumb. I know it’s a liar, but I tip my head anyway, a reflex older than language. Somewhere in the lullaby-woman’s future, a child will lose a tooth, will press it under a pillow, will believe in invisible exchanges. Somewhere in my past, I am still seven, waving at the sky from a bedroom window, positive the moon is waving back.

I step off the curb and the automatic lights click off behind me, erasing the laundromat like a stage set struck at the end of an invisible play. The parking lot smells of hot rubber and cold stars. My socks are warm inside my coat pocket, beating against my hip like second hearts. Ahead, the road is a black ribbon that someone else’s light hasn’t reached yet.

I walk toward the lie anyway, toward the place where the moon pretends to touch the earth, because even a ghost can be a compass if you’re honest about which direction you’re not going to reach.
